sources.notes: Read the full row-major submatrix query and limits: positive matrix entries up to 1e6, sum n <= 2000, and sum q <= 1e6.
Confirmed the affine local-index expansion and use of three two-dimensional prefix sums for M[i][j], i*M[i][j], and j*M[i][j].
Actually read the first substantive solution, which independently derives the row and column weight decomposition and answers each rectangle from three prefix matrices.
Re-derived the formula from the flattened local index and checked the three-rectangle-sum result against direct flattening on exhaustive small matrices and larger spot cases.

annotation.summary: For each submatrix, flatten its entries in row-major order and return the sum of each value multiplied by its one-based position in that local flattened array.
annotation.constraints: Across test cases, sum n is at most 2000 and sum q is at most 1e6, with n up to 2000 and positive entries up to 1e6. O(area) per query is impossible; O(n^2) preprocessing and O(1) queries are required, with 64-bit arithmetic.
annotation.core_idea: For a query (x1,y1)-(x2,y2) of width w, the one-based local position of global cell (i,j) is (i-x1)*w+(j-y1)+1 = w*i+j+(1-w*x1-y1). Therefore the answer is w*R(i*M)+R(j*M)+(1-w*x1-y1)*R(M), where R denotes a rectangle sum. Precompute independent two-dimensional prefix sums of M[i][j], i*M[i][j], and j*M[i][j], then evaluate all three rectangle sums in O(1).
annotation.complexity: O(n^2+q) time and O(n^2) memory per test case; O(sum n^2 + sum q) over the input.
tricks: express a query-local row-major index as an affine function of global row and column
split the weighted answer into three reusable rectangular aggregates

solution_variants.name: three-2d-prefix-sums
solution_variants.summary: Precompute 2D prefix sums for M, row_index*M, and column_index*M. Expand each local flattened position into w*row+column+constant and combine the three O(1) rectangle sums with the query-dependent coefficients.

tags.evidence: Three two-dimensional prefix-sum tables are the data structure that turns each weighted submatrix query from O(area) into O(1).
The central identity rewrites the local row-major weight as w*i+j+(1-w*x1-y1), separating every answer into row-weighted, column-weighted, and unweighted rectangle sums.
